[2018-03-14 18:15] xomnom (Xom): Hi Prismataccountants! @mtanzer  @Deleted User
[2018-03-14 18:16] xomnom (Xom): I am the coiner of the term "Barrier Value (BV)" as far as I know, on 2015 March 12. I also used the term discount factor, which equals 1 / interest rate.
[2018-03-14 18:16] xomnom (Xom): I see that in the three years I've been gone, people are now using the term inflation rate, because "inflation" is easy to type (you can't just type "interest" without confusing people); and that mtanzer has rightly complained that the term inflation rate doesn't mean the same thing as interest rate, and that "inflation" should instead refer to change in the cost ratio of gold:BV (i.e. change in the price of soak).
[2018-03-14 18:16] xomnom (Xom): BUT before we can change, there must be a change we can accept. Nobody wants to repeatedly type "interest rate". I propose the new term "tempo rate". Note that the existing term tempo has two mutually coherent meanings—
A. tempo of the game ("a low tempo situation")
B. short-term value ("you gain tempo when you buy Tia Thurnax")
—and "tempo rate" is equivalent to A (and thus coherent with B).
[2018-03-14 18:16] xomnom (Xom): Amid my figuring that out, here's something amusing I came up with:
What could "Tia Thurnax is a high tempo unit" mean? That "Tia Thurnax increases the tempo of the game"? Or that "You gain tempo when you buy Tia Thurnax" which equals "Tia Thurnax's value is in the short term" which equals "Tia Thurnax's value is high when the tempo of the game is high"? Note that "X increases the tempo of the game" and "X's value is high when the tempo of the game is high" are not equivalent! But there's a positive correlation.

--- Thread (47 messages, 2018-03-14) ---
[2018-03-14 18:57] supergreyman (Greyman): So now we'll have to start using two different terms. 😵
[2018-03-14 18:58] mtanzer: I get that the term inflation has a more global connotation.  But I still don't like using it when talking about R because inflation specifically refers to the cost of something increasing and I don't see what cost in the game is going up each turn
[2018-03-14 18:59] darkeisbein: Good and bad are the only terms you need for evaluating units :^)
[2018-03-14 18:59] 307th: well, inflation is at least loosely tied with the price of soak increasing
[2018-03-14 18:59] xomnom (Xom): basically we really need a term other than "interest rate" that people are willing to type repeatedly
[2018-03-14 19:00] supergreyman (Greyman): Let's make up our own term.

[2018-03-15 09:12] uno.pro (UnoPro): am new player confused by cosmetic system
[2018-03-15 09:15] mqp: is there some written record of that? i wonder why they thought it's better than pure RNG pack opening
[2018-03-15 09:15] mqp: i also wonder if they have some estimate of how many people, knowing that Q exists, still click to select which cards to flip
[2018-03-15 09:15] mqp: hard to measure though since how do you find out Q exists anyway
[2018-03-15 09:18] mqp: maybe, among people who have ever pressed Q, what percentage of times does anyone not press Q
[2018-03-15 09:19] mqp: that seems like the metric i want for "how much do people prefer the part where it's not just a random number that pops up"
[2018-03-15 09:20] jamesa7171: it's interesting how most of the reward system is balanced (as far as both sides of a decision being good) _except_ for flipping in the armory, where you can lose a ton of shards if you don't know what you're doing strategy-wise. Elyot mentioned once (in the comments of Coscott's rewards guide that I linked) that choosing whether to reflip in the black lab, choosing whether to exchange, choosing whether to go for infusions or black fragments, and choosing whether to spend your shards in the armory or the black lab are all decisions that are basically equivalent in expectation - so that no matter what decisions you make, you're guaranteed to not be screwing yourself over - but then the re-flipping decisions in the armory are _huge_ and new players have to seek out guides or basically risk throwing away their shards
